note: Hand-curated Opening Library for the Lasker picker. Categories are grouped by the side the user plans to play; each entry lists the first 4-6 theory plies in SAN along with a short beginner-friendly blurb. The directive 'Study this' mode walks the user through `moves` one ply at a time.

categories.label: Play as White — 1.e4
categories.blurb: Open, classical king-pawn systems. Fast development, easy to understand.

openings.name: Italian Game
openings.blurb: Points the bishop at f7 and gets pieces out fast. The textbook first opening.
openings.why: You'll practice every opening principle in one game: centre pawn, knight, bishop, castle, connect rooks.

openings.name: Ruy Lopez (Spanish)
openings.blurb: Pins the knight defending e5 and fights for the long-term initiative.
openings.why: Deep strategy but easy to start — the most-played 1.e4 reply at master level.

openings.name: Scotch Game
openings.blurb: Immediately cracks open the centre with 3.d4 and trades for fast piece play.
openings.why: You get an open position with clear plans; no endless theory memorisation.

openings.name: Vienna Game
openings.blurb: Flexible knight move. Often mutates into a King's Gambit setup with f4.
openings.why: Avoids the mainline Spanish/Italian theory and creates sharp tactical chances.

openings.name: King's Gambit
openings.blurb: Sacrifices a pawn for open lines and a direct kingside attack.
openings.why: Romantic-era attacking chess. Fun for learning initiative and piece activity.

categories.label: Play as White — 1.d4
categories.blurb: Positional queen-pawn systems. Slower, space-based plans, less forcing.

openings.name: Queen's Gambit
openings.blurb: Offers a c-pawn to divert Black's d-pawn and build a mobile centre.
openings.why: Easy plans on both sides. Famous from the Netflix series — and for good reason.

openings.name: London System
openings.blurb: Deploys the same setup vs almost anything Black plays — low-memorisation.
openings.why: You rehearse ONE setup and learn the plans deeply. Great if you hate theory.

openings.name: Catalan Opening
openings.blurb: Fianchettoed bishop on g2 aims at Black's queenside through a half-open long diagonal.
openings.why: Elegant, strategic, rich in long-term pressure. Teaches patience.

openings.name: English Opening
openings.blurb: Claims queenside space first. Often transposes to a reversed Sicilian.
openings.why: Flexible move order — you can steer into calm or sharp positions at will.

categories.label: Play as Black — vs 1.e4
categories.blurb: What to play when White opens with the king's pawn.

openings.name: Sicilian Defense: Najdorf
openings.blurb: The fighting reply. Asymmetry from move one; you play for the win.
openings.why: Top-level theory but the plans (…b5, …e5, queenside expansion) are very teachable.

openings.name: French Defense
openings.blurb: Challenges the centre and builds a sturdy pawn chain.
openings.why: Clear plans on the queenside and c-file. A reliable lifetime weapon.

openings.name: Caro-Kann Defense
openings.blurb: Supports …d5 without locking in the light-square bishop.
openings.why: Ultra-solid. You rarely get crushed and often enter pleasant endgames.

openings.name: Scandinavian Defense
openings.blurb: Hits e4 immediately and invites early trades.
openings.why: Avoids huge theory. You can play a handful of move orders against nearly anything.

openings.name: Petrov Defense
openings.blurb: Mirrors White's knight move and fights for symmetry.
openings.why: Famously solid. Good when you want a calm, equal position.

categories.label: Play as Black — vs 1.d4
categories.blurb: What to play when White opens with the queen's pawn.

openings.name: King's Indian Defense
openings.blurb: Lets White grab the centre, then attacks it with pieces and pawn storms.
openings.why: The classic attacking weapon vs 1.d4. Rich in kingside pawn storms (…f5, …f4, …g5).

openings.name: Queen's Gambit Declined
openings.blurb: Declines the pawn and holds the classical centre with …e6 and …Nf6.
openings.why: Rock-solid, principled chess. Teaches you the minority attack and central breaks.

openings.name: Nimzo-Indian Defense
openings.blurb: Pins the c3-knight and aims to damage White's pawn structure.
openings.why: A positional masterclass — bishop pair vs doubled pawns, weak squares, prophylaxis.

openings.name: Slav Defense
openings.blurb: Defends d5 with …c6, keeping the light-square bishop free.
openings.why: Very safe, lots of endgame play. Beloved by world champions.

openings.name: Dutch Defense
openings.blurb: Stakes a claim on the kingside dark squares with …f5.
openings.why: Imbalanced right from move 1. Great practice for unbalanced attacking play.
